subject: Chemistry
title: Covalent Bonds: Sharing Electrons
description: A micro-lesson that makes Covalent Bonds: Sharing Electrons usable.

hook.text: Two hydrogen atoms share their electrons to form H₂. Neither gains nor loses—they share. That's a covalent bond.

buildup.text: Each hydrogen has one electron but wants two to fill its shell. Sharing gives both atoms a full outer shell.

discovery.text: The shared electrons create a region of negative charge between the nuclei, pulling them together.

twist.text: Double bonds share four electrons (like O₂). Triple bonds share six (like N₂). More sharing means shorter, stronger bonds.

climax.text: Diamond is pure carbon with four covalent bonds per atom. This 3D network makes it the hardest natural material.

punchline.text: Sharing electrons, both atoms benefit. Cooperation at the atomic level.

quiz.question: What makes diamond so hard?
options: Every carbon atom forms four covalent bonds in a rigid 3D network
Diamond contains metallic bonds between carbon atoms
Diamond's atoms are compressed by extreme pressure underground
